teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the lab experience students are about to enter.
teaching_arc.progression: Move through course rhythm, working norms, tools, and the kinds of outcomes students will build toward.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a clear picture of how to participate effectively in the lab.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Orient students to the mobile robotics lab environment, expectations, and the workflow they will reuse across the semester.
topic_takeaways: Students should know how the lab is organized and what success looks like each week.
Students should understand the expectations for preparation, teamwork, and debugging.
Students should leave seeing the course as a build-up toward working mobile robot systems.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
transition_style: Move from the mental model to the concrete lab step students will run next, then close each section by explaining what to verify.

slides.narration_seed: Welcome everyone. Today is our kickoff session for the Mobile Robotics Lab.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Orient students to the mobile robotics lab environment, expectations, and the workflow they will reuse across the semester.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Course overview + how the lab will run so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Mobile Robotics Lab — Session 1 as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Mobile Robotics Lab — Session 1 connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Mobile Robotics Lab — Session 1 sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: Course overview + how the lab will run

slides.narration_seed: Big picture: simulation-first so we can iterate fast and debug clearly. By the end, you’ll map an environment with SLAM, navigate to goals, and avoid obstacles.
must_cover: End goal: SLAM + Navigation + obstacle avoidance
How to win: keep up weekly + document
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why Simulation? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Course overview + how the lab will run first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Course overview + how the lab will run connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Course overview + how the lab will run clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Mobile robotics is hard because the world isn’t clean. That’s why we focus on how the whole system connects.
must_cover: Real robots are messy: noise + drift + delays
We need sensing → planning → control as a system
You’ll gain strong debugging + systems skills
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Course Delivery Model (Mostly Simulation) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.story_hint: Keep Why Simulation? connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Why Simulation? clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Simulation helps you learn faster. Later you’ll see the real robot difference, but the concepts and ROS tools remain the same.
must_cover: One real-robot demo later (compare sim vs real)
Same concepts: TF, sensors, SLAM, navigation
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Final Project: House Navigator (40%) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Course Delivery Model (Mostly Simulation) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Your final project is not a real vacuum cleaner—it's TurtleBot3 in simulation—but we’ll treat it like one. The robot should move around rooms to demonstrate floor coverage while mapping and navigating.
must_cover: Robot navigates inside a house-like world (simulation)
Uses SLAM to map while exploring
Moves between rooms + reaches goal poses
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Mini-Assignments (10%): Proof you’re following the lab so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slide_ref.source_title: Mini-Assignments (10%): Proof you’re following the lab

slides.narration_seed: These are small checkpoints that show you’re keeping up. Usually you’ll submit a short proof: what you ran, what you saw (screenshot), and a couple observations.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Mini-Assignments (10%): Proof you’re following the lab to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Mini-Assignments (10%): Proof you’re following the lab is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Mini-Assignments (10%): Proof you’re following the lab in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Mini-Assignments (10%): Proof you’re following the lab connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Mini-Assignments (10%): Proof you’re following the lab accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: We standardize the stack to reduce troubleshooting chaos. If everyone is aligned, debugging becomes much easier.

slides.narration_seed: Gazebo + RViz can be heavy. If performance is an issue, tell me early—there are ways to reduce load while still meeting course outcomes.
must_cover: RAM: 16 GB recommended (8 GB may struggle)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Hardware / Laptop Requirements to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: Ubuntu: What It Is (and Why We Use It)

slides.narration_seed: Ubuntu is basically the default environment for ROS. Most docs and packages assume Ubuntu, so we reduce friction by using it.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Dual Boot Plan (Windows + Ubuntu) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Ubuntu: What It Is (and Why We Use It) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Ubuntu: What It Is (and Why We Use It) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Ubuntu: What It Is (and Why We Use It) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Dual boot is usually the best balance: keep Windows, add Ubuntu for robotics. Follow the course guide carefully to avoid common boot issues.
must_cover: Recommended: dual boot for best stability
VM can work, but may be slower for Gazebo
Follow the course install guide (don’t improvise)
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Setup Roadmap (Do this in order) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Verification is the key. If you can launch the sim, drive the robot, and see sensors/TF in RViz, you’re ready for the labs.
must_cover: 4) Verify: run sim + drive + RViz view
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Dual Boot Plan (Windows + Ubuntu) into Setup Roadmap (Do this in order) so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What Is ROS 2? (Big Picture) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Use Setup Roadmap (Do this in order) to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Setup Roadmap (Do this in order) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Setup Roadmap (Do this in order) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slides.narration_seed: ROS 2 is an ecosystem. You’ll learn the minimum concepts to build and debug a real robot pipeline.
must_cover: Framework + tools for building robot systems
We debug by inspecting the graph + data
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into ROS 2 Core Concepts (you’ll use all term) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slide_ref.source_title: ROS 2 Core Concepts (you’ll use all term)

slides.narration_seed: If you understand node/topic/service/action and how to inspect them, you can make progress fast in ROS.
must_cover: Most debugging starts with: topics + TF
slides.transition_from_previous: Use ROS 2 Core Concepts (you’ll use all term) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slides.narration_seed: We’ll practice a simple workflow: create package → build → source → run. That loop will become your default habit.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Packages, Workspaces, and colcon to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Packages, Workspaces, and colcon is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Launch files make your system repeatable. If your demo runs from one launch command, life is much easier.
must_cover: Robots are systems, not one program
Launch starts nodes + params + RViz setups
Good projects start with one command
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into RViz2 (Your Debugging Superpower) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Launch Files (Start whole systems) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Launch Files (Start whole systems) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Launch Files (Start whole systems) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Don’t guess. Look at RViz.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Gazebo (Simulation Engine) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for RViz2 (Your Debugging Superpower) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture RViz2 (Your Debugging Superpower) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Show the robot model, TF, and RViz displays as a simple pipeline so students can connect each layer to the visual outcome.

slides.narration_seed: Gazebo gives you a safe place to test ideas repeatedly. Later, real robots add more noise—but the same ROS structure applies.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into TurtleBot3 in Simulation (What We Use) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: The toughest early issue is usually dependency/version alignment. Follow the course guide so your setup matches the lab environment.
must_cover: We follow a course install guide (version alignment)
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slides.narration_seed: You don’t need to write URDF from scratch now—but you need to understand it enough to diagnose model/TF issues.
must_say: Links are rigid bodies, and joints define how those bodies connect or move.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into TF / TF2 (Coordinate Frames) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Robot Description (URDF / Xacro) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Robot Description (URDF / Xacro) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring in a TurtleBot silhouette with links and joints highlighted so the robot description reads as structure, not just XML.

slides.narration_seed: TF is a top source of bugs. We’ll learn how to inspect TF trees and fix frame problems early.
must_cover: Frames: map, odom, base_link, sensor frames…
If TF is wrong → everything looks wrong
must_say: TF2 keeps track of where one frame is relative to another frame over time.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Sensors & Data Streams (What You’ll Debug) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often confuse a change in coordinate description with a physical motion of the robot.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for TF / TF2 (Coordinate Frames) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture TF / TF2 (Coordinate Frames) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a TurtleBot or small car with attached coordinate frames so students can see relative transforms instead of imagining them abstractly.

slides.narration_seed: Most failures become obvious if you check: are topics alive, are frames correct, does RViz show sensible data?
must_cover: Debug order: Topics → TF → RViz → params
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into SLAM: What Problem Are We Solving? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Sensors & Data Streams (What You’ll Debug) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Sensors & Data Streams (What You’ll Debug) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Sensors & Data Streams (What You’ll Debug) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: SLAM: What Problem Are We Solving?

slides.narration_seed: SLAM is where the robot builds a map while estimating where it is. In this course, you’ll run a SLAM package and learn how to judge map quality and debug common issues.
must_cover: SLAM = map + pose at the same time
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.
slides.transition_from_previous: Shift from explanation into a visual or interactive view of SLAM: What Problem Are We Solving? so students can watch the concept, not just hear it.
slides.transition_to_next: After the visual explanation, step back out and explain the decision rule or takeaway students should keep.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Teach through the visual or interactive material on SLAM: What Problem Are We Solving? by narrating what students should notice and why it matters.
slides.story_hint: Keep SLAM: What Problem Are We Solving? connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand what to watch for on SLAM: What Problem Are We Solving? and why that visible behavior matters technically.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slides.narration_seed: We’ll use this as a visual reference: exploration → scans → map grows. Your project needs the same story: map first, then navigate on it.
must_cover: Watch how mapping grows as the robot explores
Notice: motion + coverage affect map quality
Goal: produce a usable map for navigation
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.

slide_ref.source_title: Navigation: From “Where Am I?” to “Go There”

slides.narration_seed: Navigation connects everything. When it fails, we diagnose which stage failed: localization/SLAM, planning, control, or costmaps.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slides.narration_seed: This is the vibe of your final demo: map ready, localization stable, goals reached consistently.
